1: The word 'that came to Jeremiah from YHWH' frames the chapter as legal covenant-prosecution. The Christological fulfillment runs through Heb 8-10: Christ is the mediator of the new covenant that supersedes the broken Sinai covenant. The repeated accusation 'they did not listen' (vv4,7,8) that triggers covenant judgment is the same hardness Jesus laments: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children... but you were not willing' (Matthew 23:37).
4: 'Obey my voice, and do all that I command you. So shall you be my people, and I will be your God.' The covenant formula (I will be your God; you shall be my people) that the Sinai covenant could not permanently secure is the content Christ comes to establish permanently. Revelation 21:3: 'Behold, the dwelling of God is with man... he will be their God and they will be his people.' The formula Jeremiah quotes from Sinai is fulfilled in the new creation.
5: 'That I may confirm the oath that I swore to your fathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey.' The land promise swore to Abraham finds its NT reinterpretation in Hebrews 11:16 and Rev 21: the patriarchs were looking for 'a better country, that is, a heavenly one.' Christ fulfills the land promise by giving not Canaan but the new creation — the 'heavenly homeland' that the material land always symbolized.
8: 'They did not obey or incline their ear, but every one walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart.' The anthropological diagnosis — the stubborn, autonomous heart — is precisely what the new covenant addresses (Jeremiah 31:33: 'I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts'). Christ came not just to announce but to enact the heart-transformation that Sinai could not produce, through the Spirit he pours out (Titus 3:5-6).
11: 'I will bring disaster upon them that they cannot escape. Though they cry to me, I will not listen to them.' This divine refusal to hear echoes in Jesus's warning about the prayer of the unrepentant (Matthew 7:21-23: 'I never knew you') and the closed door after the wedding (Matthew 25:10-12). Covenant abandonment has a real telos — the moment when mercy's time has run out — which Christ announced was coming on that generation (Matthew 23:38).
14: 'Therefore do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer for them, for I will not listen when they call to me in their trouble.' The prohibition of intercession marks the depth of the breach — but Christ's intercession transcends this. Where Jeremiah was forbidden to intercede for stubborn Israel, Christ 'always lives to make intercession' for those who come to God through him (Hebrews 7:25). The new covenant opens an intercession that the old covenant's failure could not sustain.
15: 'What right has my beloved in my house?' The beloved (yedidati) — Israel — has become unfaithful in YHWH's house (temple). Christ comes as the Son who has every right in the Father's house (John 2:16: 'Do not make my Father's house a house of trade'); he is the true yedid (beloved) who cleanses the temple and his own body becomes the true temple (John 2:19-21).
19: 'I was like a docile lamb led to the slaughter' (keves aluf yuval litvoach): one of the most direct proto-passion statements in the OT. Isaiah 53:7 uses the same lamb-to-slaughter image for the Suffering Servant, and John 1:29 identifies Jesus explicitly as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.' Jeremiah's experience of innocent suffering at the hands of his own people — from Anathoth, his hometown — prefigures Christ's rejection in Nazareth and Jerusalem. 'I did not know it was against me they devised schemes': the ignorance of the plot parallels the innocence of the Lamb.
20: 'O YHWH of Hosts, who judges righteously, who tests the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause.' Jeremiah entrusts his cause to YHWH the just judge — as Peter describes Christ's passion: 'He committed no sin... but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly' (1 Peter 2:22-23). Christ fulfills the pattern not just by suffering innocently but by entrusting himself to the Father through the passion (Luke 23:46: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit').
21: 'Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH, or you will die by our hand.' The threat of death for speaking YHWH's word — from Jeremiah's own hometown Anathoth — is a narrative rehearsal of the Nazareth rejection (Luke 4:24-29: 'No prophet is acceptable in his hometown') and ultimately of the Sanhedrin's determination to kill Jesus for his prophetic-messianic claims. The prophet's life is at stake for naming YHWH in the world.
23: The destruction of the men of Anathoth in the year of their punishment (shnat pequddatam) — the appointed accountability day — anticipates the theme of the 'day of visitation' that Jesus places in Jerusalem's future (Luke 19:44: 'because you did not know the time of your visitation'). The paquddah that Anathoth missed is the same visitation-logic applied to the generation that rejected its Messiah.
2: A theme: “Hear the terms of this covenant.” The Sinai covenant is announced and its terms proclaimed. Christ comes as the covenant mediator who does not merely announce terms but fulfills them in his own person: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them” (Matt 5:17). The covenant of Sinai, whose terms Jeremiah is commissioned to proclaim, is the shadow of the new covenant that Christ inaugurates in his blood (Luke 22:20; Heb 9:15). The command to “hear” (šimʿû) the covenant terms is fulfilled in the Shema and finds its NT form in the command to hear the Son (Matt 17:5).
3: A theme: “Cursed is anyone who does not obey the terms of this covenant.” The covenant curse is the penalty for disobedience. Christ becomes the bearer of this curse: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree’” (Gal 3:13). The curse of Jeremiah 11:3 is not a threat hanging over the obedient but the covenant penalty that the disobedient people have accumulated — and which Christ absorbs at the cross. The “cursed is anyone who” formula from Deut 27 runs through Jeremiah as a live threat and through Galatians as an absorbed judgment.
6: A theme: “Proclaim all these words in the towns of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem: Hear and obey.” The prophetic commission to preach the covenant words throughout the land anticipates the apostolic commission: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations... teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you” (Matt 28:19-20). Jeremiah preaches the old covenant words in the towns; the apostles preach the new covenant word — Christ himself — to the nations. Both are “herald” missions, and both involve the suffering of rejection by those who refuse to hear.
7: A revelation of God: “For I repeatedly warned your ancestors when I brought them out of Egypt, warning them urgently right up to this day.” The divine persistence in warning — hāšēm wᵉhāʿēḏ bᵉʾaḇôṯêhem, “I persistently warned your ancestors” — is the OT form of the Father’s patient sending: “He sent them another servant, and that one they beat; he sent still another, and that one they killed” (Mark 12:5). Christ himself is the last and greatest envoy in this long sequence of warnings — the Son sent after the servants, whose rejection is qualitatively different because of who he is (Heb 1:1-2).
9: A direct anticipation: “There is a conspiracy among the people of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.” The conspiracy against Jeremiah in this chapter (Jeremiah 11:19-21) prefigures the conspiracy against Jesus. The word qešer (conspiracy/plot) is the same word used for the assembly of those who plotted to eliminate Jeremiah (Jeremiah 11:9, 19). In the NT, the conspiracy against Jesus is described in identical terms: “the chief priests and the elders of the people plotted together to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him” (Matt 26:3-4). Jeremiah’s experience of conspiracy is a type of Christ’s Passion — the prophet whose words provoke a plot against his life.
10: A theme: “They have returned to the sins of their ancestors who refused to listen to my words.” The pattern of apostasy — receiving the covenant, then returning to the same rebellion — is the diagnostic of human incapacity for covenant keeping. This is why the new covenant in Jeremiah 31:31-34 must write the law on the heart rather than on stone. Christ is the mediator of this new covenant: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts” (Jeremiah 31:33) — made possible through the Holy Spirit who is poured out from Christ’s glorified body (John 7:39; Acts 2:33).
12: A theme: the futility of crying to the gods they have served — “they will cry out to them, but they will certainly not save them.” The idols that cannot save contrast with Christ who is able to save to the uttermost: “he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to make intercession for them” (Heb 7:25). The idol that cannot save when cried out to is the negative testimony to the necessity of the one Mediator. Jeremiah’s repeated insistence on idol-impotence (Jeremiah 2, 10, 11) is the prophetic preparation for the NT’s proclamation of the one name given among men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).
13: A shadow: “as many gods as you have cities, O Judah; as many altars to Baal as streets in Jerusalem.” The multiplication of worship objects — localized, plural, competing — is the inverse of the one God in one temple in one city. Christ consolidates and fulfills this singularity: there is one God and one mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5). The replacement of Jerusalem’s many Baal altars with the one altar of the cross shows how the divine judgment of Jeremiah 11 is not merely destructive but preparatory — clearing the ground for the one offering that actually avails.
16: A shadow: “the LORD once called you a flourishing olive tree, beautiful with fine fruit; but with the roar of a great storm he will set it on fire, and its branches will be broken.” Paul uses precisely this olive-tree image in Romans 11 — Israel as the cultivated olive tree, with some branches broken off, and Gentiles as a wild branch grafted in. The burning branches of Jeremiah 11 become the broken-off branches of Romans 11:17. Christ is the root that supports the tree: “do not boast over those branches... it is not you who support the root, but the root that supports you” (Rom 11:18). The olive-tree judgment of Jeremiah opens into the olive-tree mercy of Romans.
17: A theme: “The LORD of hosts, who planted you, has pronounced disaster on you because of the evil that the house of Israel and the house of Judah have done.” The parable logic — the planter who planted his vine/tree and finds it faithless — runs from Ps 80 through Isa 5 to Jer 11-12 to Jesus’ parable of the vineyard (Matthew 21:33-41). In each case, the owner of the vineyard/tree expects fruit, finds faithlessness, and pronounces judgment. Christ embodies both the Owner who pronounces judgment and the new Planting — the true vine (John 15:1) who produces the fruit that Israel failed to yield.
18: A revelation of God: “The LORD revealed it to me, and I knew; you opened my eyes to their actions.” The prophetic knowledge of hidden conspiracy comes by divine revelation, not human intelligence. This is a pattern that reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who “knew all people” and “did not need anyone to tell him what was in a person, for he himself knew what was in a person” (John 2:24-25). Jesus knows the conspiracy against him before it unfolds (John 13:1-3; Jeremiah 18:4); his foreknowledge is not merely prophetic warning but omniscience — YHWH’s own attribute of knowing all hearts (Acts 1:24).
22: A theme: “their young men will die by the sword; their sons and daughters will die by famine.” The judgment on those who conspired against Jeremiah extends to their households. This principle of covenant consequence — that the covenant penalty reaches those who reject the prophets — finds its most serious expression in Christ’s pronouncement over Jerusalem: “The blood of all the prophets shed since the foundation of the world will be charged against this generation” (Luke 11:50). Those who reject the last and greatest Prophet bear the accumulated weight of all prophetic rejection. The judgment on Anathoth’s conspirators is a small-scale enactment of this larger covenantal logic.
